title: Hávamál
titleEn: Hávamál — stanzas 1–2 (Sayings of the High One)
draftNote: Pilot — the first two stanzas. The wording is well attested, but the exact orthography and lineation are still being checked sign-by-sign against Gustav Neckel's 1914 printing; small changes are possible before the full poem is prepared.
source: Gustav Neckel, Edda: Die Lieder des Codex Regius nebst verwandten Denkmälern, 1st edition (Heidelberg: Carl Winter, 1914), Hávamál, stanzas 1–2. Text in normalized classical Old Icelandic following Neckel's conventions — þ, ð, æ, œ, the nasal vowel ǫ, and acute marks for vowel length. Eddic poems are cited by stanza, not line, so the margin numbers are stanza numbers. Pronunciation convention: reconstructed classical Old Icelandic, c. 1200. Translations made fresh from the original. (Pilot: orthography and lineation to be verified against the Neckel printing before the full Gestaþáttr.)
about: The Hávamál (‘Sayings of the High One’) survives in a single medieval manuscript, the Codex Regius (GKS 2365 4to, c. 1270); everything printed here is a modern editor's normalization of that one source, and the standard spelling itself — including the letter ǫ and the acute length-marks — is a scholarly convention, not something the scribe wrote consistently. The poem is gnomic: loosely linked proverbs of hospitality, caution, and conduct, voiced as if by Odin. These are its first two stanzas — the wary advice a traveller gives himself on entering a stranger's hall. The numbers in the margin are stanza numbers, the usual way of citing Eddic verse.

sentences.t:
Gáttir allar,
áðr gangi fram,
um skoðask skyli,
um skygnask skyli;
því at óvíst er at vita,
hvar óvinir sitja á fleti fyrir.
sentences.l: All the doorways, before one walks forward, should be scanned, should be spied out; for it is uncertain to know where enemies sit on the hall-floor ahead.
sentences.i: Before you step through any doorway, look it all over, scout it well — for you can never know for certain where enemies may be sitting in the hall ahead of you.
sentences.n: The poem opens in ljóðaháttr, the ‘chant meter’ of Old Norse wisdom verse: short paired lines whose stressed syllables alliterate (Gáttir–gangi; skoðask–skygnask). You will see it throughout. Three grammar habits begin here. skyli ‘should’ is the gnomic subjunctive of skulu — it gives proverbs their timeless ‘one should…’. skoðask and skygnask carry the -sk middle ending (worn down from sik ‘oneself’), here roughly ‘be scanned / look about one’. And um before a verb is an old, now-meaningless particle — ignore it in the sense. flet (dat. fleti) is the raised plank-floor along a hall's walls where people sat, so á fleti fyrir = ‘on the benches ahead.’

sentences.t:
Gefendr heilir!
Gestr er inn kominn,
hvar skal sitja sjá?
Mjǫk er bráðr,
sá er á brǫndum skal
síns um freista frama.
sentences.l: Givers, hail! A guest is come in — where shall this one sit? Very hasty is he who, at the fire-logs, must make trial of his own worth.
sentences.i: Good health to the givers! A guest has stepped inside — where is he to sit? The man who must prove his worth at the hearth-fire stands there in an anxious hurry.
sentences.n: A greeting to the hosts: Gefendr ‘the givers’ (of hospitality), heilir ‘hale, whole’ — the ancestor of English hail and wassail. inn is the adverb ‘in’ (gestr er inn kominn, ‘a guest has come in’); sjá here is the demonstrative ‘this one’ (nom.), not to be confused with sá ‘that one, he’ in the next line. Note the letter ǫ in mjǫk and brǫndum — the normalized spelling of a nasal o-vowel (later Icelandic ö); use the ‘þ→th’ button to fold it to a plain o. The second half is genuinely uncertain: á brǫndum is literally ‘on the firewood/logs’, taken as ‘at the hearth’, and síns um freista frama ‘to make trial of his own worth’ — the exact picture is debated. We keep the literal image and flag the doubt, as this text will do throughout.

glossary.gáttir: gátt (f.) — doorway, door-opening; here acc. pl., ‘all the doorways’
glossary.allar: allr — all, every; here nom./acc. pl. fem., agreeing with gáttir
glossary.gangi: ganga — to go, walk; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, ‘(before) one goes’
glossary.um: um — here an old expletive particle placed before the verb, with no meaning of its own (elsewhere a prep., ‘about, around’)
glossary.skoðask: skoða — to look at, examine; here with the -sk middle ending, ‘be examined / look over for oneself’ (pres. subj.)
glossary.skygnask: skygnask — to look about, peer around, spy out (-sk middle verb; pres. subj.)
glossary.skyli: skulu — shall, must, ought; here 3rd sg. pres. subjunctive, ‘should’ (the gnomic ‘one should’)
glossary.því: því — for, because — því at = ‘because, for’ (því is the instrumental of the demonstrative sá/þat)
glossary.at: at — that; to (before an infinitive); in því at = ‘because’
glossary.óvíst: óvíss — uncertain, not known; here neut. sg. óvíst (ó- ‘un-’ + víss ‘certain’)
glossary.er: vera — to be; here 3rd sg. pres. ‘is’ (er also serves as the relative particle ‘who, which, that’)
glossary.óvinir: óvinr (m.) — enemy, foe (ó- ‘un-’ + vinr ‘friend’); here nom. pl.
glossary.sitja: sitja — to sit; here 3rd pl. pres. ‘(they) sit’
glossary.á: á — on, upon, at (prep. with dat. or acc.)
glossary.fleti: flet (n.) — the raised plank-floor / benches along a hall's walls; here dat. sg., ‘on the floor’
glossary.fyrir: fyrir — before, in front, ahead (adv./prep.); á fleti fyrir = ‘on the benches ahead’
glossary.gefendr: gefandi (m.) — giver; here nom. pl. gefendr, ‘the givers’ (of hospitality), i.e. the hosts (present participle of gefa ‘to give’)
glossary.heilir: heill — hale, whole, sound; as a greeting ‘hail!’; here nom. pl. masc., addressing the hosts
glossary.gestr: gestr (m.) — guest, stranger; here nom. sg.
glossary.inn: inn — in, inward (adverb of motion): gestr er inn kominn = ‘a guest has come in’ (distinct from the article inn/hinn)
glossary.kominn: koma — to come; here past participle masc. sg., ‘come’ (er kominn = ‘has come’)
glossary.skal: skulu — shall, must; here 3rd sg. pres. ‘shall, is to’
glossary.sjá: sjá — this, this one (demonstrative pron., nom. sg. masc.); identical in form to the verb sjá ‘to see’, but here the pronoun
glossary.mjǫk: mjǫk — much, very, greatly (adv.); normalized spelling with ǫ (later Icelandic mjög)
glossary.bráðr: bráðr — hasty, impetuous, in a hurry (adj.); here nom. sg. masc.
glossary.sá: sá — that, that one; he (demonstrative pron., nom. sg. masc.); sá er = ‘he who’
glossary.brǫndum: brandr (m.) — firebrand, burning log; here dat. pl. brǫndum, ‘at the fire-logs / hearth’
glossary.síns: sinn — his own (reflexive possessive); here gen. sg. masc., ‘of his own’, going with frama
glossary.freista: freista (+ gen.) — to try, test, make trial of (infinitive)
glossary.frama: frami (m.) — advancement, success, distinction, worth; here gen. sg. frama, object of freista
